title: typeof — narrow primitives
instruction:
The `typeof` operator narrows the type of a value inside a conditional block. TypeScript uses this to know exactly what type you're working with.

Write a formatter that handles multiple types.
successMessage: Inside each case TypeScript narrows the type — `.toFixed()` is only available in the `number` branch.

title: instanceof — narrow class instances
instruction:
`instanceof` checks if a value was created by a specific constructor. TypeScript narrows to that class inside the block.

Handle different error types with instanceof.
successMessage: Each `instanceof` check gives access to the class-specific properties — `.field` for ValidationError, `.statusCode` for NetworkError.

title: in operator — check for property existence
instruction:
The `in` operator checks if a property exists on an object. TypeScript narrows the type based on the property check.

Distinguish between response types using in.
successMessage: The `in` operator narrows the type by checking for a property. Combined with the `type` discriminant, you get precise narrowing.

title: Custom type guard — is keyword
instruction:
A type guard function with `value is Type` as the return type tells TypeScript to narrow the type at the call site when the function returns true.

Write reusable type guards for API responses.
successMessage: Type guard functions check at runtime AND tell TypeScript the type is safe after the check. `r.email` is accessible inside `isUser(r)` blocks.

title: Assertion functions — throw or guarantee
instruction:
An assertion function throws if a condition isn't met. After calling it, TypeScript knows the condition holds.

Write assertion functions for input validation.
successMessage: After `assertString(raw)`, TypeScript knows `raw` is `string` — `.length` and `.trim()` are available without casting.

title: Discriminated union narrowing
instruction:
A discriminated union uses a shared literal field (`kind`, `type`, `status`) as a discriminant. TypeScript narrows automatically in switch statements.

Handle different payment method types.
successMessage: Each `case` narrows to the specific union member — `pm.last4` is only accessible in `case "card"`. Add a new union member and the switch will warn you about an unhandled case.

title: Narrowing with equality checks
instruction:
TypeScript narrows types based on equality comparisons. Checking `x === null` narrows `x` to `null` in that branch, and to the non-null type in the other.

Safely access a nested nullable object.
successMessage: After `if (cfg.database === null)`, the `else` branch knows `cfg.database` is `{ host: string; port: number }`. TypeScript tracks the narrowing on both sides of the check.

title: Control flow analysis — unreachable code
instruction:
TypeScript performs control flow analysis — it tracks which values are possible at each point in the code. This lets it catch unreachable code and exhaustive checks.

See how TypeScript tracks control flow.
successMessage: TypeScript tracks that after the `null` check, `score` must be `number`. After each `return`, narrowing continues — by the last `return "F"`, TypeScript knows `score` must be 0-59.

title: Narrowing with Array.isArray
instruction:
`Array.isArray()` is a built-in type guard — TypeScript narrows to `T[]` when it returns true.

Process a value that might be a single item or an array.
successMessage: `Array.isArray()` is a type guard — inside the `if` block, TypeScript knows the value is an array. This is a common pattern for API endpoints that accept either a single value or a batch.

title: Never — exhaustive union handling
instruction:
`never` is the empty type — a value that should never exist. Use it in the `default` case of a switch to make TypeScript error when a union member isn't handled.

Build an exhaustive status handler.
successMessage: If you add `"archived"` to `TaskStatus` and forget to add its `case`, TypeScript errors on `const exhaustive: never = status` — because `status` would be `"archived"`, not `never`. Compile-time exhaustiveness checking.

title: Type narrowing in callbacks
instruction:
Type narrowing works inside callbacks too. TypeScript tracks the narrowed type through function boundaries when the narrowing is definitive.

Filter an array to a specific subtype.
successMessage: `Extract<Animal, { kind: "dog" }>` extracts just the dog variant from the union. After filtering, `dogs` is typed as the dog variant — `.breed` is accessible without further narrowing.

title: Narrowing with truthiness — null and undefined
instruction:
Checking a value's truthiness narrows away `null`, `undefined`, `0`, `""`, and `false`. TypeScript understands all truthiness checks.

Build a safe property accessor.
expectedOutput: Web: https://alice.dev | Twitter: @alice_dev | Bio: Senior TypeScript developer who l...
successMessage: Truthiness checks narrow away `undefined` — inside `if (user.website)`, TypeScript knows `website` is `string`. The `!user.bio` check narrows to the falsy path (no bio set).

title: Using satisfies for narrowed object types
instruction:
`satisfies` validates an expression matches a type while preserving the narrowest possible inferred type. Use it when you need both validation and precise types.

Build a type-safe configuration with satisfies.
successMessage: `satisfies ThemeConfig` validates the structure without widening the types. `theme.colors.primary` is `string` and `theme.fontSizes.body` is `FontSize` — both validated, neither unnecessarily widened.
